data.title: Arista Networks: All Ready To Storm To New Highs (Upgrade)
data.description:
Arista Networks: Networking Is The Next Choke Point

For those of you who thought that the AI boom was mostly about NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), a timely article penned by CEO Jensen Huang provides important insights into elaborating why the AI infrastructure buildout is expected to broaden and proliferate across the economy.

In the now-famous “AI is a five-layer cake” analogy, he provides us with a pictorial representation of where Nvidia’s opportunity set currently is and where it has also expanded into, capturing economics across the layers that align with its core competencies. And one of them is arguably in the networking space, right?

Within the five-layer cake, Nvidia enunciates why networking falls within the infrastructure labeling, but isn’t the only thing that belongs to that layer, but nonetheless is pivotal to where Nvidia believes it is competitive and well positioned to gain share and expand its growth pillars:

The above chips are infrastructure. This includes land, power delivery, cooling, construction, networking, and the systems that orchestrate tens of thousands of processors into one machine. These systems are AI factories. They are not designed to store information. They are designed to manufacture intelligence. - Jensen Huang on “AI is a five-layer cake.”

So the AI chip race that has been dominated by none other than Jensen Huang and his team is expected to feature networking more prominently from here, as the company faces questions on the potential for market share losses to custom chip archrivals like Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) or even its merchant chip peer, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD). It is definitely not a coincidence that Nvidia wants to convince the market that networking has become the most important growth driver, taking over from its compute dominance.

While networking accounted for just over 20% of its compute revenue base, it grew by an astounding 270% over the past one year. Therefore, Nvidia’s ability to bundle its networking platform together with its GPUs and CUDA software libraries is a formidable go-to-market strategy that could have significant implications for Arista Networks, Inc. (ANET).

As for ANET, while it’s still the Ethernet market leader, Nvidia’s rapid growth has really caught the eye of the market. I guess I don’t have to impress upon you that the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) since my last update in September 2025. Back then, while I was impressed with the growth profile of ANET, aligning closely with the pace of the AI buildout, valuation concerns held me back from assigning a more optimistic rating to the stock. While the underperformance since that update is not glaring, it’s still a noteworthy development, considering that the stock is still up more than 65% in the past year.

So it does seem like investors are considering possibly even tougher competition against Nvidia, even though I must highlight that management did put up a courageous front, in reminding us that networking is a broad spectrum that Nvidia doesn’t fully address with its products and solutions, of which Arista is well positioned to capture the economics:

They've (Nvidia) been very successful at the back-end networks that interconnect GPUs. But our Cloud Titan customers, certainly including Meta, have much more broad and diverse networking needs than that. So if you're talking about the backbone, talk about the data center spine. You're talking about the access networks. That's a place where I think Arista is well differentiated, and it's very strong. - Arista Networks, Inc. Presents at Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026 Transcript.

Arista Networks: Growth Expected To Stay Resilient

I think management provided clear insights into why Arista’s hardware/software integration offers customers a clear differentiation lever over peers, and Arista’s consistent revenue growth corroborates why the firm lifted its guidance to more than 25% for FY2026, as the AI infrastructure buildout continues at an unrelenting pace. As a result, I think ANET bulls will want to remind us that networking could be the next big chokepoint in the AI boom, as it has since moved from chips to storage, spurring the memory stocks to spectacular outperformance that surpassed even my most optimistic imagination.

And Arista is arguably firmly established on scale-up, scale-out, and scale across workloads, so it’s not difficult to envisage that the company’s networking technologies will remain dominant, which have already been validated by its main hyperscaler customers like Meta and Microsoft. Hence, I expect Arista’s solutions to be front and center as the AI data centers expand to “massive scale,” benefiting from Arista’s hardware (scalable architecture), and enabled by its EOS capabilities for “intelligent real-time load balancing that accounts for actual network utilization to uniformly distribute traffic and avoid flow collisions.”

Therefore, I think the six-month consolidation in its price action appears ready for the next advancing phase, once the market gets past the initial hesitance over its well-deserved premium valuation. As AI clusters become increasingly prolific, as well as larger, high-quality networking technologies will undoubtedly feature even more prominently in big tech’s discussion.

Nevertheless, I think that the threat from white box solutions could still hamper a more aggressive bullish rerating in ANET, eating into its margins profile. Yet, the simplicity and scalability of Arista’s software platform should help allay fears, as hyperscalers still rely on ANET’s domain expertise, although we cannot rule out the need for big tech to optimize their CapEx spending with less expensive white box solutions if their homemade software platform manages to improve over time, which could lead to higher potential share losses to these providers.

Buy Or Sell ANET Stock?

For a stock that’s valued at 38x forward earnings, I will be disingenuous if I mention that ANET stock isn't expensive. It’s still well over the 21.8x sector median, expressing the high level of optimism that the market has baked into its growth momentum. Yet, the wisdom of the market suggests to me that buyers are not unduly worried about whether ANET could deliver in the current context, where AI networking has proven to be a highly lucrative opportunity, as we saw with Nvidia’s rapid growth rates.

While competition has always been intense, I think ANET’s upgraded outlook, coupled with the bullish print from its price action, corroborates my decision to view its prospects with a more optimistic lens than before. And when I consider that ANET’s valuation has reverted closer to its 10-year average of 35x, from a high of 50x back in September, I think the market is setting up very nicely for a well-deserved advancing phase once the consolidation comes to a conclusive end.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Comcast Looks Intriguing Here, But History Remains A Concern
data.description:
There are a number of ways to make the bull case for Comcast Corporation (CMCSA) stock here. Shares have already bounced 26% from a 10-year low reached in late October, but on a consolidated basis they remain cheap relative to earnings and cash flow. The core Residential & Connectivity Platforms segment, which drove ~two-thirds of Adjusted EBITDA in 2025, offers a turnaround case. Comcast is revamping its go-to-market strategy and pricing in a bid to recapture customers lost to competition.

In the remaining part of the business, there are potential "hidden asset" arguments for assets like Universal Studios and the theme park business, both of which ostensibly should receive far higher valuations than does Comcast as a whole. That in turn might suggest a "sum of the parts" case, which too argues for solid upside for Comcast on paper. And ostensibly, the arguments for the two sides of the business should work together: a scenario where sentiment improves toward the core broadband offering should see investors also give greater focus to (and have stronger confidence in) the rest of the portfolio.

It's a tempting case, certainly. But that's almost part of the problem. The history of the past 15 years or so shows that these tempting cases so rarely play out. Consumer-facing businesses dealing with secular challenges have seen their valuations come in and thus seem to provide opportunities, particularly relative to historical valuation. That trend has held across multiple industries: packaged food, alcoholic beverages, legacy media, and retail, to name just a few.

Very few of those cases have actually worked out. Most legacy businesses that manage secular change, unsurprisingly, have not been able to navigate well. It's possible Comcast is an exception to the trend, but at least for now it seems a bit too early to bet aggressively that it will be.

The Comcast Turnaround

Looking to 2025 results, Comcast stock looks exceptionally attractive. Shares trade at about 5.4x last year's Adjusted EBITDA, and more importantly, barely 5x 2025 free cash flow. Tax benefits related to the spin-off of Versant (VSNT) depress the latter's multiple, but even adjusting for those benefits, Comcast is still priced for declining profit going forward.

There's certainly an agreement that the outlook is too pessimistic. 2025 performance was soft but hardly disastrous: revenue was flat, and Adjusted EBITDA declined less than 2%. The Peacock streaming service is still running losses—per earnings releases, Adjusted EBITDA for the platform in 2025 was negative $1.08 billion—and simply getting that to break even adds something like 5-6% to tax-normalized free cash flow. ($800 million after-tax improvement on a current ~$15 billion FCF base.)

So, very simply, the case here is basically that if Comcast can deliver stable free cash flow, the stock potentially has double-digit annualized upside. Roughly speaking, even a 10x P/FCF multiple suggests three- to four-year total returns of 60% at least (a ~$160B market cap would be a 44% increase, while the dividend adds ~4% a year and buybacks/deleveraging potentially more). In a world where Comcast can convince the market its business has improved, triple-digit total returns absolutely are on the proverbial table.

And the best way to convince the market is to get the RCP segment fixed. Here, too, it's not as if results have been disastrous: Adjusted EBITDA increased each year from 2021 to 2024 (at a ~2.8% annualized rate) before declining last year. More pressure is coming in 2026 as the company reworks pricing and promotions—but the hope is that those efforts can jumpstart growth and fend off competition from 'overbuilders' and fixed wireless offerings.

As co-chief executive officer Mike Cavanagh put it at a conference this month, "We had pricing that was less transparent and more harder [sic] to engage with for the consumer with more difficulty getting product onboarded than the new competition, particularly fixed wireless brought." Basically, for lower-usage customers (and I saw this firsthand with a family member who switched away from a different broadband provider), moving to fixed wireless was a simple choice. Pricing was lower (at least upfront), and installation was/is pretty much painless.

By streamlining pricing, offering a new five-year price guarantee, and making the overall process less complicated, Comcast thinks it can win in those markets. And, as Cavanagh has noted, the fact that it held essential monopolies in so many markets means it has exceptionally granular data on the customers who left for fixed wireless or fiber and thus can put together offers to win those customers back.

For higher-usage customers, meanwhile, broadband should remain superior to fixed wireless, and there too better marketing and simplified pricing should help Comcast compete. The bull case for CMCSA and Charter Communications (CHTR) before the full arrival of fixed wireless was that growing data demands (streaming video, Internet of Things, etc.) would keep them insulated from wireless competition, and for some users it seems likely that outlook will hold.

Comcast also is offering wireless plans of its own, which offer the potential to eventually add profit while also strengthening retention in broadband. Many of the lines in service now have been given for free; as customers move toward paid plans (later this year), Comcast should see revenue growth. At the very least, costs to serve free users should go away (though management has said the offering already is profitable), reversing some of the profit hit expected for the segment this year.

Beyond Broadband

Meanwhile, there are valuable assets beyond the core business. The Theme Parks segment generated just over $3 billion in Adjusted EBITDA in 2025 (up 25% from 2019 levels, which admittedly is fine but not spectacular). Valuing that business is tough (the same problem holds for the SOTP cases for Disney (DIS)), but at a 10x-plus multiple, it seems more than reasonable. (Six Flags (FUN) is above 8x, and that company is a mess.) That covers ~30% of the current market capitalization and provides growth opportunities even if connectivity remains stagnant.

As a number of analysts have noted, the Studios business has real value as well. Adjusted EBITDA was just over $1 billion in 2025, but the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) suggests the Universal assets would be exceptionally valuable to the right owner. The Media segment does offset some of this potential upside (profits are declining, and even with the weaker networks shunted off to Versant, the outlook is not particularly bright), but overall, a rough SOTP also suggests a path toward double-digit annualized gains:

  • RCP: $22 billion in 2026 EBITDA (-15%) at 5.5x (CHTR is at 5.7x 2026E) gets to $121 billion;
  • Business: $5.7 billion in 2026 EBITDA (flat) at 6x (the trend has been better than residential), for ~$34 billion;
  • Media: $2.9 billion in 2026 EBITDA (-10%) at 3x (see peers), for ~$9 billion;
  • Theme Parks: $3 billion-plus in 2026 EBITDA (small growth) at 12x, for ~$38 billion;
  • Studios: $1.3 billion in 2026 EBITDA (strong slate should drive higher profits), at 15x (trophy asset?), getting to ~$20 billion.

That admittedly rough (but often conservative) SOTP puts enterprise value at about $222 billion, which would get the market cap to $132 billion. That suggests about 19% gains to a CMCSA stock price of $36. And there is upside there, particularly if the RCP turnaround can hold (note that our model uses 2026 profits, which are ostensibly somewhat depressed).

Can This Case Work?

So on paper, there is an argument that the rally from October lows can continue. And while "on paper" is a huge caveat there (in investing in general and particularly for this kind of name), forced to choose, I'd rather own CMCSA than short it. New blood in the top ranks can probably help, particularly given that the Roberts family had run Comcast for over six decades. There's no doubt room for improvement in terms of execution, and there is perhaps the potential for further financial engineering after the VSNT spin. (That spin has not gone that badly, given that it was clearly why Comcast wanted that business off the books.)

But at the same time, it's worth reiterating how much history works against the bull case here. For essentially the entire 21st century, few companies have been able to avoid being disrupted and outcompeted by new entrants. And, again, that's particularly true in established, consumer-facing industries. There have been strong cases on paper for AT&T (T) and General Mills (GIS); for the Detroit automakers, and for so many retailers. So few of these plays, with familiar names but a risk that their best days have passed, have worked. (The one obvious success is General Electric (GE); I'm likely forgetting at least a couple of others.)

And given how much pressure is on such a huge proportion of EBITDA (Media plus RCP is three-quarters of total profit), the core risk here is that history repeats once again. The combination of two competitors keeps a lid on pricing. Meanwhile, costs aren't stable because, at the same time, Comcast needs constant network improvements to keep pace. (This is basically the AT&T/Verizon (VZ) problem in wireless.) Theme Parks is good but not spectacular or big enough to offset profit declines or create shareholder value in a transaction. CMCSA maybe provides some income but underperforms the market.

Again, there are reasons to think that Comcast can chart a different path than many companies in similar situations. But those reasons, for now, remain relatively speculative (particularly in terms of the turnaround efforts in broadband), and this may be a case where it's wiser to own at $35 with signs of success than at $30 when the outlook remains uncertain. Comcast's strategy can work, but putting money behind it requires believing that it likely will work—and we're just not quite there yet.

data.title: After A Massive Rally, The Easy Money May Be Gone - Prologis
data.description:
Prologis, Inc. (PLD) currently sits in our neutral range for valuation. I think that’s fair. Here are a few factors to consider:

  • Shares (at $132.05 intraday) trade at 27.63x forward AFFO consensus estimates of $4.80. This is on the high end for REITs presently.

  • Shares are about 47.8% above their lowest close over the last 52 weeks.

  • Shares are about 7% under their highest close over the last 52 weeks (set in early March 2026).

  • Dividend yield is at 3% with about 4.9% guidance for growth in Core FFO per share excluding net promote income this year.

  • We want to pay strong attention to the growth. However, we are focused on long-term growth rates while acknowledging that short-term growth rates can have a large impact on the multiple.

  • Prologis has rallied all the way from our “Strong Buy” range to our “neutral” range. We currently have a neutral rating on PLD. (For reference, our last article was here).

Here’s a chart showing the historical price and multiples:

Using the share price or the forward AFFO multiple, we saw the valuation move up quite a bit.

Prologis has delivered a strong rally over the last year, with shares climbing substantially in roughly the last 11 months. That performance has pushed the valuation higher and took our valuation from strong buy to our neutral area. With the stock already reflecting some optimism, it becomes important to evaluate the latest operating results and the outlook for continued growth.

Prologis Core FFO And AFFO Results And Guidance

Prologis results:

  • Core FFO of $1.44 matched estimates of $1.44. Excluding net promote income, $1.46 would beat estimates.

  • AFFO per share of $1.20 beat estimates of $1.15 by $.05.

Here’s the big chart:

We also track the guidance for Core FFO Ex Net Promote Income because I find that value is more useful for reflecting the performance of the real estate portfolio:

Note: We could use the term “Net Promote Expense.” For simplicity, I list it as “income” even if we have a negative value. Over long periods the value will usually be positive.

Looking To 2026

  • Consensus estimate was $6.13.

  • Guidance for Core FFO was $6.10 (lower by $.03).

  • Guidance for Core FFO Ex Net Promote Income was $6.15 (higher by $.02).

That’s implying growth continues at about 5%. Absent any negative shocks, we may see PLD beat the original guidance as they did last year. For 2025 they ended up about 1.5% above the original guidance.

Guidance for same-store net operating income is important also. It is one of the major factors we consider when evaluating long-term growth rates and the appeal of the underlying real estate.

That’s slightly faster growth in cash NOI than PLD saw in 2025.

General Notes

The major criteria continue to look good.

Average occupancy guidance has a midpoint of 95.25%, similar to the best quarter for PLD in 2025. This matches commentary from much earlier (several quarters ago) about when occupancy was expected to hit the low point and the rebound we would most likely see.

Rent change on renewals (leasing spread) is coming down but is still quite solid. If this trend continues, it could pressure same-store net operating income. But we’re still seeing healthy performance for PLD.

Interest Rates

Like many REITs with operations around the globe, PLD has utilized lower interest rates in Europe for a greater portion of their debt recently. This mitigates part of the pressure from higher rates. They also have a reasonably long weighted maturity on their debts at 8.5 years. However, long-duration (like 10-year Treasury) interest rates are currently trending higher in the United States. That is generally negative for valuation.

Over the last several days, we’ve also seen short-term rates jumping higher.

Data Centers

We may see PLD putting about 40% of their new development into data centers (based on the PLD Q4 2025 earnings call).

I’ve mentioned before that data centers can be built at fairly high yields, which is quite attractive. It creates a potential challenge down the road from excessive development, but it makes them attractive development projects today. This is still a minor portion of PLD’s portfolio, but they’ve evaluated the land in their portfolio and found many cases where they can optimize the value by building data centers (instead of industrial). This can also be used for attracting investor capital into the development funds Prologis manages. I like this method of exposure to data centers much more than I would like owning data center REITs. PLD is still an overwhelmingly industrial REIT with a great balance sheet and expertise in development.

My Expectations

I’m inclined to think PLD will slightly outperform guidance so long as the economy doesn’t go off the rails. That’s pretty good. They keep the balance sheet strong and deliver steady growth over time. The performance of the underlying portfolio backs the growth in AFFO per share. However, valuation has been pushing quite a bit higher. I’m not attracted to the current multiple.

With occupancy averaging about 95% over 2025, PLD has some room to grow there. Management is great at forecasting global development and demand levels. I expect them to be right. However, I find the projections they provide typically are usually designed to be slightly cautious, so they are less likely to have something “go wrong.” Management calls for global warehouse occupancy to increase slightly over the year, with rent growth emerging towards the end of the year.

Conclusion

AFFO per share solidly beat estimates, but AFFO can be a bit volatile because of the timing for maintenance capex. In this case, I think AFFO introduces more volatility for PLD than it does for some of the non-industrial REITs.

Guidance was roughly on par with estimates, depending on how we adjust for the negative $.05 related to net promote income. I see that as a non-issue. So I don’t think anything here should land as a positive or negative surprise.

Recent Trade

I believe it is important to disclose trading activity and to provide evidence for trades. If investors don’t track their positions, they can end up with some delusional ideas about their performance. We recently closed out the vast majority of our position in Prologis. The trade was provided to subscribers with a real-time trade alert:

To enhance transparency, I also believe it is important to include screenshots showing confirmation of a trade. Some people say there is no reason to do that. I do it for two reasons:

  1. Many investors have told me that it is easier for them to be clear about my outlook when they can see the trade.

  2. It establishes a definitive statement about the actual execution. Typos happen. Miscommunication happens. The screenshot prevents miscommunication. Either that trade happened exactly as shown, or the analyst faked the screenshot and should be held to account.

Therefore, here is the screenshot:

Here are the returns on the shares I sold:

data.title: SentinelOne: Contrarian Buying Opportunity
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc. (S) reported its fourth-quarter and full-year fiscal 2026 results on Thursday, March 12, which highlighted that the cybersecurity platform achieved for the first time more than $1.0B in annual revenues. The company’s annual recurring revenue business is booming and exceeded more than $1.1B in annual recurring revenues in Q4 '26.

Since adoption of AI applications in the cybersecurity market is growing rapidly, SentinelOne is eyeing a massive TAM expansion in the years ahead, which is set to lift shares up in the long term, in my opinion. Recent concerns about the impact of AI on software companies also seem to be exaggerated, making a contrarian entry into SentinelOne even more attractive.

Robust Q4 '26 performance

SentinelOne on Thursday reported fourth-quarter earnings that beat consensus estimates on earnings. Specifically, the cybersecurity firm’s normalized earnings were reported at $0.07 per share, beating the estimate by $0.01 per share. Analysts projected a top line of $271.2M, which SentinelOne missed only marginally, by $91K.

AI-driven cybersecurity platform: surging ARR and improving non-GAAP earnings

SentinelOne is a cybersecurity company that is focused on the enterprise market and runs chiefly its autonomous, AI-native security ecosystem, which is called the "Singularity Platform." The key product offer here is the provision of behavioral threat detection utilizing the company's unified, "data-first" architecture. The company provides services across the entire cybersecurity spectrum, with core services including endpoint security, cloud security, identity security, and “Purple AI,” which is the SaaS platform's agentic AI service.

SentinelOne's business is on fire, and the company achieved major top and margin milestones in FY 2026. The cybersecurity platform generated $271M in total revenues in the fourth quarter, showing 20% year-over-year growth. The company’s total revenues reached $1.001B in FY 2026, exceeding the firm’s revenue milestone of $1.0B in annual revenues for the first time. The annual recurring revenue business, which extrapolates the Q4 ’26 top line to the full year, reached $1.11B, showing 22% year-over-year growth.

SentinelOne has strong retention rates in its core SaaS business, although they slightly dipped in the fourth quarter. Retention rates are important key performance metrics for software and SaaS companies, including the cybersecurity market, because they show investors to what extent existing enterprise customers are ramping up their platform spend from one reporting period to the next. In the fourth quarter, SentinelOne generated a net retention rate of 109%, showing a 3 PP drop compared to the year-earlier period. This figure means that customers ramped up their spending by ~9% compared to the same period last year, on a like-for-like basis.

SentinelOne generated an operating income margin of 5.7% in the fourth quarter, showing 4.5 PP margin growth compared to last year. The increase in margins is driven by a higher number of lucrative enterprise customers on the firm's books—SentinelOne business customers generating $1M+ ARR increased 20% Y/Y. The company is increasingly targeting Fortune 500 companies in its acquisition strategy, which is what could drive additional operating income margin gains in FY 2027 (the current fiscal year).

TAM expansion a catalyst for growth

According to Precedence Research, the cybersecurity market is set for rapid growth in the next decade, and artificial intelligence is a crucial catalyst in driving the expansion of the industry. Based off of estimates of the market intelligence firm, AI in cybersecurity is expected to become a $168B market by 2035, which calculates to an annual average growth rate of 19% over the next decade.

Near 50% discount to historical P/S ratio...

SentinelOne’s shares have suffered valuation pressure recently as investors have started to panic about the adverse impacts of AI. While artificial intelligence is obviously boosting sales of AI accelerators and GPUs in the chip industry, the utilization and potency of AI in the software market has led investors in recent months to focus on the disruptive impact of this particular technology.

As a result, shares of SentinelOne have revalued lower by approximately 29% in the last year, leading to the cybersecurity platform trading at a significant discount to its historical valuation ratio: SentinelOne has a 3-year average price-to-revenue ratio of 6.1X, which means shares can currently be scooped up at a 46% mark-down to this historical valuation ratio, mainly because of growing, and in my opinion unfounded, SaaS concerns. CrowdStrike (CRWD), a major competitor in the cybersecurity space, is trading at a forward revenue multiplier of 15.6X, so shares are significantly more expensive than those of SentinelOne.

In my opinion, SentinelOne, while being the much smaller company based off of revenue volume, has upside potential in 2026 related to the above-discussed TAM expansion and surge in non-GAAP operating income margins. Customer acquisition also looks good, especially in the lucrative $1M+ ARR market. I believe a revaluation of the historical P/S is possible, under the condition that SentinelOne continues to grow at annual rates of 20% in the future and expands the reach of its Singularity platform. A 6.1X P/S ratio for SentinelOne implies a fair value of ~$25 per share, signifying 86% upside revaluation potential.

Primary risks for SentinelOne

SentinelOne is growing rapidly in the market for cybersecurity solutions, in large part due to the use of AI. While I generally see artificial intelligence as a positive advancement for the software, cybersecurity, and agentic AI industries, the market currently has a different attitude and has chosen to adopt a more bearish stance.

While AI lowers the costs of software development as well as the entry barriers into the industry for new software/cybersecurity start-ups, I believe established cybersecurity platforms like SentinelOne, with a demonstrated expertise in the enterprise segment, will continue to be able to sell their services in the market. What may change my mind about SentinelOne is if the company were to see a slowdown in its ARR growth rate below 20% or if its operating income margins dropped to below 5% going forward.

Final thoughts

SentinelOne is growing extremely fast, and the company had a landmark year in 2025 by posting for the first time in its history a more than $1.0B total revenue volume. The cybersecurity platform is benefiting from an expansion in the size of the cybersecurity market, driven by artificial intelligence deployment across the enterprise landscape, which creates a secular catalyst for sustained top-line and earnings growth in the future.

SentinelOne is trading ~46% below its historical P/S ratio, indicating undervaluation due to recently emerging concerns about the impact of AI on SaaS companies. With the market and the firm's ARR surging and margins improving, I believe the risk profile is skewed to the upside here, and I initiate the cybersecurity platform as a Buy.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: War, Oil, Airlines, Energy, AI, Private Credit
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Oil price volatility, airlines lower, energy up (0:25) Banking stocks down; private credit concerns (2:50) FOMC meeting next week and beyond (4:50) FedEx, Oracle earnings (7:10)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup. Always great to have you.

Talk to us. We missed last week. We have a war going on longer than many have anticipated. Is oil top of mind for you?

Brian Stewart: Oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) were hovering in the low 60s level in early February. Now they're in the mid 90s. They have at times pushed above 100 though haven't really stayed there for any meaningful amount of time.

Predictable results of that, airline stocks are lower. The airline ETF (JETS) is down about 2% this week about 18 % in the past month. Honestly, airlines are kind of running into a perfect storm. There's the higher oil prices.

Meanwhile, the war itself is causing travel disruptions in the region. And then you have at the same time, the DHS shut down, which isn't supposed to affect TSA and those kinds of things, but is a lot of reports of long lines for checking and things like that. So you just have a lot of stuff going down in the airline industry at the same time.

So you have a stock like Southwest (LUV), which is down 5 % this week and down about 25 % in the past month. The stock was at a new high before we kind of hit the recent turbulence. (UAL) is down 2 % this week, down about 20% for the month. So no real surprises there in a situation where oil prices spike, you always look to the airlines first as kind of the canary in the coal mine, the first affected.

Meanwhile, it's been kind of a jamboree for energy stocks so far in the last couple of weeks, kind of expected there as well. They were already seeing a lot of attention. I'm talking about the energy sector writ large was already seeing a lot of attention with the, kind of understanding that the AI build out is going to be an energy bonanza.

They're going to need a lot of power to run the data centers. And so energy stocks have been seeing a lot of attention lately. Specifically oil stocks recently, obviously with the conflict in Iran.

You have Chevron (CVX) up 3% for the week, up 7% for the month, up 28% year to date. Exxon Mobil (XOM) up about the same amount year to date. Chevron reached a new high recently. Exxon Mobil is bouncing around near its highs.

In the oil sector, it's going to be a little bit of a stock picker situation just because different companies are going to have different exposure to the conflict. But certain energy companies, certain oil companies, it's just the higher prices is just kind of gravy at this point.

Rena Sherbill: And what else are you looking at in the market?

Brian Stewart: I think the other key topic is the banking stocks have been down recently. This is kind of more private credit concerns. JP Morgan (JPM) this week, marked down loans in its private credit groups, that caused concern. Honestly, there hasn't been a huge reaction in the market.

JP Morgan is back a little bit from where it was earlier in the week. It's now basically flat for the week. But it's down about 5 % for the month. Going through some of the bigger ones, Wells Fargo (WFC) down 13 % for the month, you have Goldman Sachs (GS) down 12%.

So you're seeing concerns building over the last few weeks that there's kind of a ticking time bomb in these private loan funds that are out there.

Interestingly, this is true about the war as well. If you look at the performance of the S&P 500 (SP500) out of context, just look at the list of the daily performance of the S&P 500. Nothing seems out of place at all. I think the S&P has been down like five or the last six sessions, something like that, as we're speaking now, but they're all very, very modest declines. It's still kind of hovering in the same range it's been in since November. We're only about five percent off of highs for the S&P 500.

Meanwhile, you have continued chatter about whether or not we're in an AI bubble. We have a murky to worrisome job market. We have inflation being sticky above 3 % with more inflation pressures coming from energy on the way. You have all these things going on and the market has been pretty resilient. So the question becomes is kind of a whistling past the graveyard kind of situation or is the market right to not overreact to these situations?

Rena Sherbill: What would you say about the upcoming FOMC meeting and what may happen to rates? Whether or not that's affected by what's happening with oil prices and inflation at large and what are your thoughts there?

Brian Stewart: So as it stands, the market is showing a 99 % chance that rates are going to stay the same. Honestly, that's true for a pretty long horizon. So looking to the April meeting, it's a 93 % chance that rates will be the same after that meeting. Looking to June, it's 74%. It's a 35 % chance that we're not going to get any cuts at all this year. And if those come, those are going to come after September, like in the September meeting or after that's kind of what the market is betting right now.

We got PCE numbers that came in recently showing core PCE. That's a that's the fed's favorite inflation measure. So core PCE was up three point one percent in January. The again, the Fed's target is two percent. So not only is it above, it's a full percentage point plus above I think the market had kind of gotten used to, okay, we might not be able to get to 2%, but 2.4, 2.5, we can kind of live with it.

3% is getting back to the point where you kind of have to put your inflation fighting hat back on. It's complicated by the fact that we're to get a change over in the leadership at the Fed. We're still waiting for Senate approval of that, but as soon as that comes, you just don't know what the personality of the new Fed chief is going to be.

Trump obviously has been pushing for lower rates very aggressively this entire year and even before. So his handpicked new Fed chief is probably leaning in that direction just in terms of getting picked for the job. But with inflation high, it's not clear that that's what's going to happen.

I think for the near term, we have kind of pretty good idea of what's going to happen just because the markets are betting that there's practically no chance that we're going to get a cut this time around.

But I think there's some uncertainty that starts to build later in the year and we'll just have to follow the numbers and see what happens.

Rena Sherbill: Anything else macro-wise worth pointing out for this week or next?

Brian Stewart: Besides the Fed decision coming next week there's not that much to keep an eye on. Crude inventories has now become a crucial economic measure. So I think it'll be important to look at that. That comes weekly, comes on Wednesday.

Looking ahead to like at earnings, you have FedEx (FDX) reporting, FedEx is in a bucket of other companies that are being affected by oil. FedEx is down about 7 % in the past month, which is better than the rival (UPS), which is down about 18 % in the past month. So obviously those are companies that rely heavily on fuel to do their job. So it'd be interesting to see FedEx's commentary on that.

Just while we're on the topic, other companies that are sort of outside the airlines that are being hurt by the oil thing. Carnival (CCL), which is also reporting next week, cruise line, it's down 24 % in the past month. So getting its commentary will be critical next week as well. It's a good data point in terms of just general, what are executives thinking about in terms of the current oil situation.

And then while we're just talking about like individual companies and earnings, we should mention Oracle (ORCL) reported this week. So the stock popped 9% after its earnings, strong earnings and guidance also provided an update on its spending and funding plans.

One of the worries about Oracle was the same kind of worry that was happening with all the hyperscalers, just that they're spending so much on this AI build out that maybe it becomes sort of a pyrrhic victory to even win the AI fight.

Stock was up sharply, but in context, it really wasn't that impressive a move. The stock reached a 52 week high in September around $345. It's now trading at 156. So well, well, well below that high. It's not even back to where it was in late January, still down about 20 % for the year.

While I think you should see that 9% from Oracle is kind of a relief rally, this doesn't look like there's a bubble bursting anytime soon, but optimism at least for that stock is still well off of levels it was kind of late last year.

So I think we're still in kind of a wait and see situation with the AI stocks, whether or not, especially with energy prices going up, you've got economic uncertainty, there's still worry about the general logistics of the AI build out itself, whether or not all that money is gonna be worth it, whether or not they can even get it done.

So I think that's been the motor for the market over the last couple of years. And right now we're just waiting for the next data point to figure out what's next.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ascent Industries: Back In Buy Territory After Its Strategic Reset
data.description:
Ascent Industries Co. (ACNT) is no longer a mixed chemicals-and-tubing business but a pure-play specialty chemicals company. They now focus on supply solutions for industrial customers. And, operationally speaking, ACNT has a hybrid model. Basically, they function as a manufacturing partner for its customers, while also generating revenues from owning the chemical formulations that it produces. However, ACNT is undergoing a transition period where it has to improve its product mix, utilization, and margins after exiting its legacy tubular operations. This does create uncertainties for the stock, but at its current valuation multiples, I feel there’s an interesting bull case to be made at these levels.

Industrial Chemicals

Ascent Industries Co. is now a pure-play chemicals company that develops products for industrial end markets. ACNT was incorporated back in 1958 as the successor to Blackman Uhler Industries, Inc., which was a chemical manufacturing business founded in 1945. ACNT is currently headquartered in Schaumburg, Illinois, and operates three facilities in Cleveland, Fountain Inn, and Danville. I previously covered ACNT last July, and since then, the stock has appreciated by as much as 35%. However, this month the stock has quickly given back all of those gains, and it's down 27% from its recent highs, so I thought it was timely to update my thesis on this name.

As a quick overview, ACNT supplies tailored chemical solutions, which are used across a wide range of applications. For example, ACNT’s products are useful for households, industrial cleaning, coatings, oilfield chemicals, agrochemical formulations, metalworking fluids, water treatment, pulp and paper, construction, automotive, and industrial textiles. Still, ACNT as a whole has one single reportable segment called Specialty Chemicals, which has two sub-business categories.

ACNT’s first business category is its custom manufacturing. Here, ACNT’s customers own the formula or intellectual property, and ACNT acts as the manufacturing chemical partner. This means ACNT has to develop the process and scale it from the laboratory or pilot stage to commercial volumes, and then manufacture the product. During 2025, this was ACNT’s largest revenue contributor, which generated approximately $52.6 million in sales. On the other hand, ACNT’s second business category is its core technology products. ACNT owns the formula or the IP and manufactures the product to be sold to customers. This business generated only $22.3 million during 2025, but it’s clearly nonetheless a valuable part of its operations.

On the bigger picture, ACNT actually positions itself as a chemical-as-a-service company. After all, they have dedicated production lines for particular clients, and also systems that are multi-purpose and can be used across different jobs and industries. Their equipment and facilities give them substantial flexibility to pursue a range of projects without needing specific facilities for each one. And, in that sense, I feel their business will always remain relevant to some niche in one way or another because specialty chemicals are always in demand in modern societies. Thus, ACNT’s underlying value proposition here is having the technical expertise and resources/production means to produce them, which is ACNT’s bread and butter.

Ascent's Structural Reset

With that in mind, ACNT’s 2025 was mixed overall. Last year, net sales fell to $74.9 million from $80.8 million in 2024. However, gross profit rose to $17.2 million from $10.7 million, and gross margins improved to 23.0% from 13.2% in 2024. Similarly, adjusted-EBITDA improved to -$0.6 million from -$4.7 million in 2024. Still, it’s worth highlighting that ACNT’s Q4 was slightly weaker than the rest of the year, as its gross margins contracted to 18.3% from 19.2% (Q4 2024). So, I imagine this is partly why the market has turned negative on the stock, as Q4 2025 could suggest the business is deteriorating right now.

In fact, ACNT’s management itself stated that Q4 was difficult. Yet, they doubled down on their earnings model, which they believe has improved as their pivoting towards a specialty chemicals operating model. And, it’s important to remember that their focus on specialty chemicals required them to sell their legacy tubular operations to Bristol Metals (BRISMET) and American Stainless Tubing (ASTI). Obviously, this strategic transition involved not only the sale of the tubular business but also the stripping out of leftover equipment and assets, which I think added to its Q4 headwinds.

On top of that, ACNT had to improve its portfolio mix to help keep its operating margins high. Yet, this required them to balance between holding out for only high-margin transactions and potentially leaving some of its production capacity idle, or take some lower-margin volumes if it helps absorb fixed costs and keep its plants fully operational. Given its transitional stage, ACNT ultimately decided to accept some lower-margin orders to increase its volume at lower prices. And this is how you can explain why Q4’s margins seemed to deteriorate as well.

Additionally, ACNT had to deal with the elimination of legacy assets that were not contributing positively. Concretely, ACNT had cut out its legacy tubular facility in Munhall, Pennsylvania. This was a pipe and tube facility that kept costing them maintenance CAPEX until ACNT could reassign the lease to a new tenant in late 2025. But overall, as you can see, ACNT’s current challenges center around how it’ll continue changing its product mix, while managing costs and legacy expenses.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ACNT currently trades at a $120.5 million market cap. Its latest 2025 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $57.6 million in cash against $0.4 million in notes payable (aside from other regular operating liabilities). In total, its book value reached $87.0 million, suggesting a reasonable P/B of 1.4. And using these figures, I estimate ACNT’s EV at roughly $63.3 million, which actually makes it even more compelling. After all, ACNT generated around $74.9 million in revenues during 2025, which would price it at a cheap EV/S of 0.8.

For comparison, the sector’s median EV/S is much higher at 2.0. So, overall, I think ACNT’s valuation at these levels seems quite undervalued using these multiples. Likewise, I don’t think ACNT’s cash burn should be an issue right now. I calculate that they burned through $2.0 million in 2025, which seems quite manageable compared to their cash reserves. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And this figure implies a comfortable cash runway of 28.8 years, so despite the recent stock price plunge, ACNT’s financing shouldn’t really be an issue for the foreseeable future at its current burn rate.

Naturally, ACNT is not without its risks. For starters, it’s a microcap with low trading volume of only 148.0 thousand shares. At the current $12.90 PPS, this implies a shallow trading liquidity of $1.9 million, which means even tiny buy/sell orders can easily push the stock price significantly. So, you should expect the stock to remain volatile for the foreseeable future. However, unlike the first time I covered ACNT, the stock is now much closer to a fully completed pure-play specialty chemicals business. Its balance sheet is also much cleaner, and we’ve already seen they can indeed continue operating under this leaner model. Back then, this was a major uncertainty that deserved a discount in the stock price.

Therefore, at this point, the main risk is whether or not ACNT’s management can continue executing under this leaner model. And I believe Q4 2025 was a reminder that quarterly results can still get ugly. However, what gives me optimism about ACNT going forward is that the consolidated 2025 adjusted-EBITDA for the full year is almost breakeven at this point. Its adjusted-EBITDA margin improved significantly from -5.8% to -0.8%, and if this trend continues into 2026, it would suggest that this year will be a turning point for ACNT. Of course, betting on turnarounds is always inherently speculative, but on balance, I feel there’s a viable “Buy” case for ACNT at these levels.

Conclusion: Back In The “Buy” Zone

Overall, I feel ACNT is back again in a very compelling valuation range. I believe its business prospects aren’t as bad as its multiples might suggest. In fact, I think the company is well-positioned to continue delivering consistent shareholder value over time after it fully completes its transition towards a leaner specialty chemical producer. Naturally, this bull case may take longer to play out than investors may like. However, in the long run, I deem ACNT stock offers a compelling risk-reward profile for new investors at these levels. Just make sure you size your position accordingly, as my bull case hinges on management diligently executing on their renewed strategic vision without any meaningful setbacks.

data.title: The Social Security Trust Fund Is Rapidly Approaching Insolvency
data.description:
Introduction

I’m old enough to remember when Al Gore coined the term “lockbox” as a euphemism for how he was going to protect the Social Security surplus.

The year was 2000, and Gore was the incumbent Vice President, campaigning for the presidency. His administration had just recorded a budget surplus of $236 billion, the largest in American history still to this day. More than two-thirds of the surplus, or $160 billion, was created by the revenue from Social Security taxes exceeding payments to Social Security recipients.

Vice President Gore wanted to save that Social Security surplus for the future, while his opponent, then Texas Governor George W. Bush, wanted to return the surplus to American citizens through tax cuts.

Gore was memorably parodied by Darrell Hammond on Saturday Night Live, a performance that helped turn the word “lockbox” into a lasting piece of SNL lore.

Unfortunately, American retirees will not be laughing now.

CBO Forecasts That The Social Security Surplus Will Be Depleted By The End Of Fiscal Year 2031

On Wednesday, Congressional Budget Office Director Phillip Swagel testified before the Senate Finance Committee about the fiscal outlook over the next ten years. As he has for the past several years, Swagel stressed how our expenditures continue to exceed our revenues, causing our debt to GDP to balloon. He warned the lawmakers, again, that the trajectory of budget deficits was unsustainable.

Of particular interest to me, though, was Swagel emphasizing that Social Security’s Old Age and Survivors Insurance (OASI) Trust Fund would be exhausted by the end of fiscal year 2031.

That is only 5 years away!

As recently as 2022, when CBO published a similar ten-year fiscal outlook, the forecast was that the Trust Fund would last until 2035.

Even one year ago, CBO projected that OASI wouldn’t become insolvent until the end of Fiscal Year 2032.

Over the past 4 years, the expected end of OASI has been shortened by four years. This is an extremely rapid deterioration in the Social Security program.

Why Social Security Is Deteriorating

The Social Security Act was passed in 1935, during the Great Depression, as part of President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s New Deal. There was widespread unemployment and poverty in our country, and the Act was created as a contributory system that would provide future retirement benefits for workers.

The Old Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund was established in 1939 as the depository of all Social Security payroll taxes collected by the government. After benefits were paid, the surplus was invested in non-marketable U.S. Government bonds.

Everyone who ever received a paystub has been surprised to see that their take-home pay was much less than the gross amount they earned for the pay period. They knew that they would have state and federal income taxes withheld from their check, but then there was this mysterious FICA tax as well.

The Federal Insurance Contributions Act, or FICA, is a mandatory federal payroll tax deduction of 6.2% of earnings to fund Social Security. This amount is matched by employers.

There have been many changes to Social Security over the years to extend its solvency. The Social Security Amendment of 1983 accelerated payroll tax increases and gradually raised the full retirement age from 65 to 67. This led to a significant increase in the Social Security surplus.

The fundamental problem with Social Security, though, is a demographic one. We have an aging population, and the ratio of workers paying taxes to retirees receiving benefits is shrinking. In 1940, shortly after the program was created, there were many workers and few retirees who had paid into the system. The ratio started at 159 to 1.

As the program matured, the ratio narrowed. By 1960 the ratio was 5.3 to 1, and gradually it has declined to the current ratio of 2.8 to 1. By 2035 it is expected to fall further to 2.2 to 1.

The impact of this demographic shift is that current Social Security revenue is no longer large enough to cover current Social Security expenses. Hence, to meet the shortfall to pay retirees their earned benefits, Social Security must dip into the OASI Trust Fund.

Annual Social Security expenses began exceeding annual revenues in 2021. This was in part due to an acceleration in Cost-of-Living Adjustments tied to the then 40-year high CPI increase. That the Fed has not been able to bring CPI back down to its target 2% rate has exacerbated the problem.

Last summer’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act increased the deficit further by reducing total revenue targeted for Social Security.

As seen in the above chart, the annual Social Security deficit is growing rapidly; hence, it needs to reach for the Trust Fund to cover the shortfall. But, as stated earlier, the Trust Fund will only be able to cover Social Security benefits for 5 more years.

What This Means For Retirees

Americans depend on, and rely on, Social Security. Currently there are almost 70 million retirees collecting Social Security benefits. Of these people, almost half receive at least 50% of their household income from Social Security, and 25% count on Social Security for 90% of their income.

In fiscal year 2032, if nothing is done, OASI will become insolvent, and retirees will see an automatic drastic cut in their Social Security benefits. The agency will only have current income to meet expenses,

The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget estimates that in this situation, benefits will be cut by 24% across the board. Such an action would be devastating to the neediest of families, leading many to sink deeper into poverty.

There would be a ripple effect throughout the economy. Consumer spending would drop, and growth would slow. Plus, strains would be placed on other government-funded social service programs, both federal and state.

It is simply an untenable situation.

What Must Be Done

This is a five-alarm fire situation that has significant long-term costs for our country. Yet, it is not well known.

Yes, everyone sees that Social Security is the single largest program in the federal budget, accounting for 22% of total federal spending, but the severity of the problem has not been widely discussed. And time is of the essence.

What should be done is something similar to what was done the last time Social Security expenses exceeded Social Security revenues on an annual basis. That period occurred in the late 1970s. When President Ronald Reagan came into office in 1981, he created the National Commission on Social Security Reform. This was a 15-member bipartisan group tasked with solving the severe pending crisis with Social Security.

The negotiations were difficult and tense, but ultimately a compromise was reached. The Social Security Act of 1983 was passed and has sustained Social Security for the past 43 years. The time is now for an update.

Conclusion

CBO Director Phillip Swagel testified before the Senate Finance Committee regarding the fiscal outlook over the next 10 years, and what stood out to me was the pending insolvency of the Social Security Trust Fund by the end of fiscal year 2031.

In 2021, annual Social Security expenses began exceeding revenue, requiring dipping into the OASI Trust Fund to cover the shortfall. This operating deficit will continue to grow based on CBO projections, and the Trust Fund will become insolvent in the next 5 years.

If nothing is done, Social Security recipients will see their benefits slashed by 24% across the board, according to the Committee for a Responsible Budget.

This will have a devastating impact on the 70 million Americans who count on Social Security. Benefits currently represent 50% of household income for almost half of retirees, and 25% of retirees count on Social Security for 90% of their income

A drastic cut to benefits will send many to deep poverty, cause consumer spending to slow, weaken the economy, and put a strain on other state and federal social service agencies.

Something needs to be done, on a bipartisan basis, to solve this imminent Social Security crisis.

Where is the lockbox when we need it?

data.title: Myers Industries: Growth In Industrial And Infrastructure Supports Margins, But Premium Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Myers Industries, Inc. (MYE) is expected to drive revenue growth from end markets such as Industrial and Infrastructure. However, the Food & Beverage end market should be a headwind to net sales in the coming quarters. On the profitability front, the cost-saving program and favorable mix should be accretive to margins in FY26. Based on my relative model, I believe the stock is trading at a premium of ~19%. Although I like the growth prospect of the company, I believe the forward earnings growth is already priced into the current valuation.

Business Overview

Myers Industries is a manufacturer of polymer products and material handling solutions based in the United States. The company engages in the production of plastic containers, pallets, fuel containers, and protective packaging, which are used in various end markets, including Infrastructure, Industrial, Vehicles, Food & Beverage, and Consumer. The products of the company help in supporting storage, transportation, and logistics in supply chains. The company primarily operates in North America and focuses on higher-margin growth products.

Revenue Analysis & Outlook

In FY2025, the net sales were $825.7 million, a decrease of 1.3% year-over-year or down 0.6% year-over-year excluding the impact from exiting the lower-margin products. The decline was due to the weakness in the distribution segment. However, the growth from the Material Handling segment provided support to net sales in FY2025. In the Material Handling segment, the net sales from Industrial and Infrastructure end markets were up year-over-year. But the sales were down year-over-year from the consumer and Vehicle end markets.

Looking ahead, I believe the Industrial and Infrastructure end markets should support the revenue in the coming quarters. However, the Food & Beverage end market should be a headwind in revenue growth. Moreover, the Vehicle end market is expected to remain stable in the future. The revenue should be impacted by the exit of rotomolded plastic products. In the recent quarter, the company discontinued the lower-margin products, particularly rotomolded plastic products, and idled two facilities in Ohio. This implies a net sales loss of $5 million per quarter or $20 million in the fiscal year.

The overall demand in the Industrial end market of the company is linked with manufacturing activity. The US Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) is a leading indicator of manufacturing activity in the country. PMI was at 51.6 points in February 2026, above the benchmark of 50.0 points. But, down from the January PMI data. The February PMI was supported by increased output and new orders. However, the demand was impacted by higher prices, tariff impacts, and bad weather conditions. Despite weak momentum in February, business owner sentiments were strengthened, driven by plans of business expansion and the launch of new products. Additionally, rising geopolitical tension and military conflicts should increase the defense budgets, particularly among the US and NATO countries. This should increase the demand for the defense products of the company in the coming quarters. Although the Industrial end market is largely cyclical, I believe the improving manufacturing activity, coupled with increasing defense demand, should provide support for the revenue from this segment.

The growth in the Infrastructure end market should remain strong, driven by robust spending on Infrastructure and an increasing addressable market. In the recent earnings call, management mentioned increasing demand from the growth in hyperscale projects and data center construction. Hyperscaler projects are the large-scale infrastructure projects to support cloud computing, AI workloads, and other services. The hyperscaler projects are created by the Big Five, including Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) or Google, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Oracle Corporation (ORCL). The capex by the Big Five hyperscalers is expected to exceed $600 billion in 2026, an increase of 36% year-over-year. This should be driven by increased investments in AI infrastructure and data center expansion. Additionally, there is an ongoing shift in the market from the traditional wood matting to durable composite matting, which should increase the Total Addressable Market (TAM) of Myers' products. Currently, the backlog for the matting products is at a record level, and the backlog-to-sales conversion provides strong momentum entering FY2026.

The Vehicle end market of the company deals in recreational vehicles, marine, and automotive component packaging. The demand for recreational vehicles and marine remains subdued due to the lower consumer spending in the market. A higher interest rate environment led to lower spending on discretionary purchases by customers. Although the Federal Reserve has implemented rate cuts previously, the long-term borrowing costs are still elevated. At present, the U.S. 30 Year Treasury Yield is at 4.8%, up ~ 20 basis points year-over-year. This could impact the financing of discretionary purchases like recreational vehicles and marine products. However, the management believes that the market should be stabilized gradually with the improving consumer confidence.

The Food & Beverage end market of the company engages in agricultural storage solutions, which are used to transport and store crops. The demand in this end market is influenced by the agricultural industry cycle. Currently, the agricultural industry is operating at the lower end of its cycle due to weak commodity prices and lower farmer spending. This should weigh on the Myers’ revenue in the near term. However, as agricultural conditions improve, Myers, with its important role in agricultural logistics through seed distribution and bulk material handling, should deliver good results in the long term.

Margin Analysis & Outlook

In FY2025, the adjusted gross margin stood at 33.7%, an increase of 30 basis points from the previous year, supported by the lower material costs, favorable cost productivity, and favorable sales mix. Moreover, the adjusted operating margin was at 10.3%, an improvement of ~30 basis points year-over-year, driven by the cost-saving benefits from the Focus transformation program.

The margins of the company should improve in FY2026, supported by the cost savings from the Focus transformation program, a favorable revenue mix, and improved facility utilization through exiting lower-margin products. However, the potential headwinds should be pricing dynamics, higher input costs (particularly resin), and operational deleverage from the weakness in end markets like Food & Beverage and Vehicle.

The continued efforts on the cost-saving program, i.e., the Focus transformation program, have already helped the company in generating approximately ~$20 million in annualized cost savings. The program includes structural Selling, General, & Administration (SG&A) cost reduction and efficiency initiatives. Moreover, the company has discontinued the production of low-margin products and exited two rotational molding facilities, which should improve the cost efficiency and enhance the manufacturing utilization across the remaining footprint. Additionally, the company is also focusing on higher-margin products growth, such as infrastructure matting, industrial solutions, and other defense products.

Despite these improvements, some factors should weigh over margins in the coming quarters. Resin is the main input for plastic manufacturing, which is expected to remain elevated. It is expected to negatively impact the margins if the cost increases are not fully offset by the pricing actions and productivity gains. Pricing in the market is dynamic as the higher input costs are pressuring the manufacturers to increase the price. But the highly competitive landscape is limiting the company’s ability to pass on increased input costs to customers. Additionally, the demand in the end markets, such as Food & Beverage and vehicles, is expected to be weak, which should limit volume growth and operational leverage.

Valuation

Myers Industries is currently trading at a non-GAAP P/E (fwd) of 16.69x with a consensus EPS estimate of $1.26. The major end markets, including Industrial and Infrastructure, are expected to support the revenue in the coming quarters. Moreover, the other end markets, like Vehicles, are expected to stabilize in the future. However, the Food & Beverage end market should be challenging due to lower demand in the agriculture industry. With key end markets expected to remain healthy, I believe the company should report increased revenue on year-over-year basis in FY2026. On the profitability front, the cost-saving programs and favorable revenue mix should be accretive to the overall margins. But the margin expansion depends on pressure from the high input price and pricing actions. Furthermore, I have created a relative valuation model to compare the company along with its peers based on revenue growth rate, operating margin, and P/E ratios. Looking at the revenue growth outlook, UFP Industries is expected to decline by 0.13%, which is lower than the mean of the peer forward revenue growth rate of about 3.19%. Moreover, on the profitability front, the operating margin (TTM) of the company stood at 10.33%, which is more than the peer median margin of approximately 6.65. Comparing Myers’ P/E ratio of 16.69x with its peers, it is above the median P/E ratio of 14.00x.

Risk

My investment thesis assumes moderate growth from the Industrial end market. However, if the macroeconomic conditions deteriorate, it could negatively impact the results from the industrial end market of the company. Additionally, if the high-interest rate environment continues, it could further pressure the discretionary spending of consumers. This may pose a headwind for sales in the Vehicle end market. The impact of price/cost dynamics is uncertain as the material input costs increase, but the company is reluctant to implement price hikes due to increased competition. This uncertainty could weigh on the company's results more than expected.

Conclusion

While it is true that the majority of the company’s end markets, including Infrastructure and Industrial, are expected to support the revenue, I believe the macroeconomic uncertainty may weigh on the near-term performance. Moreover, the pricing power is limited by a highly competitive landscape, and pressure remains from high-input costs. Based on consensus EPS estimates and a 5-year average FWD P/E ratio of 15.04x, the company is trading at a premium of approximately 11%. Moreover, compared to the sector median P/E ratio, the company is at a ~19% premium despite Myers’ lowest forward revenue growth among its peers. Therefore, I believe investors should wait for a better entry point and assign a Hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Invesco Rising Dividends Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Invesco Rising Dividends Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio positioning

Compared to the Russell 1000 Index, the fund is generally balanced across sectors and industries, with exposure to areas that have been benefiting from long-term secular growth tailwinds, including e-commerce, electric vehicles, cloud computing, industrial automation, medical technology and broadband. Importantly, our dual focus on companies generating sustainable levels of free cash flow and having healthy balance sheets gives the fund a quality bias and provides defensive characteristics that we believe should prove valuable if market volatility persists.

Notable Additions

Linde (LIN) was purchased due to its essential and resilient business model, anchored by long-term contracts and leadership in industrial gases, which we believe positions it to benefit from megatrends like global energy transition and technology growth.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT) was added to the fund due to its diversified medical technology leadership, robust new product pipeline, including the upcoming Libre CGM sensor partnership with Medtronic (not a fund holding), and a favorable legal position in infant formula litigation. Despite near-term headwinds, we believe Abbott’s solid fundamentals and growth prospects position it well for long-term value.

Republic Services (RSG) is one of the largest US waste management companies and was added to the fund due to what we saw as its attractive risk/reward profile, solid execution and rising consensus estimates. With its consolidated industry structure, high barriers to entry, strong recurring revenues and compelling free cash flow yield, we believe Republic Services offers potential for stable growth, dividend increases and share repurchases, making it a defensive, value-oriented holding.

Notable Sales

Oracle (ORCL) is a major US technology company providing database software, cloud infrastructure and enterprise applications. We sold it due to concerns about downside risk as its investment in AI infrastructure has caused rising debt and negative free cash flow.

Stryker (SYK), a medical technology company, was sold because we believed the risk/reward profile had become less attractive as its valuation rose.

Smurfit Westrock (SW), a packaging company, was sold due to deteriorating fundamentals that have seemingly led to negative investor sentiment and uncertain timing for the stock to recover despite what we consider an attractive free cash flow yield.

Performance highlights

The fund delivered a positive return and performed in line with the Russell 1000 Index during the quarter. Strong stock selection in the communication services, financials, industrials and real estate sectors added the most to relative performance.

Stock selection in the information technology, health care, energy and utilities sectors detracted from relative results.

Contributors to performance

Alphabet reported strong quarterly results, beating expectations across all key metrics, with accelerated growth in Search, YouTube and Google Cloud. Google Cloud’s backlog surged 82% year over year, driving improved operating margins.

Eli Lilly’s stock surged during the quarter, driven by strong momentum in its GLP-1 obesity drug franchise, widespread analyst upgrades, strategic product pipeline partnerships and speculation about acquisitions, culminating in the company reaching a $1 trillion market capitalization. These factors, combined with robust earnings and apparent optimism toward future therapies, propelled the stock higher.

Apple’s stock rally was driven by record iPhone 17 sales, all-time high services revenue, profit margin expansion, earnings that beat expectations and upbeat management guidance, which appeared to fuel investor enthusiasm about AI, customer loyalty and the company’s resilient ecosystem.

Detractors from performance

Microsoft reported strong revenue and earnings that beat expectations. However, the stock lagged during the quarter, apparently due to supply constraints amid stronger-than-anticipated demand for data center capacity that limited near-term upside. Growth in Azure and commercial cloud bookings remained robust, but the supply-demand imbalance is expected to persist through the fiscal year.

Oracle’s stock underperformed due to concerns about its heavy AI infrastructure spending, amid surging capital expenditures and upward revisions to annual spending forecasts. Despite a large backlog of AI and cloud contracts, doubts about the timing of revenue realization and potential margin and debt pressures from elevated spending led to stock volatility and a sharp pullback.

Meta delivered strong revenue growth but the stock price lagged amid concerns about higher AI-related capital expenditures and operating expenses, significant losses in Reality Labs and a large one-time tax charge that pressured earnings. These factors appeared to raise fears about near-term profitability and free cash flow, seemingly overshadowing growth momentum and leading to cautious guidance.

About Risk

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

Common stocks do not assure dividend payments. Dividends are paid only when declared by an issuer’s board of directors and the amount of any dividend may vary over time.

The Fund's value may be affected by changes in the stock markets. Stock markets may experience significant short-term volatility and may fall or rise sharply at times. Adverse events in any part of the equity or fixed-income markets may have unexpected negative effects on other market segments. Different stock markets may behave differently from each other and U.S. stock markets may move in the opposite direction from one or more foreign stock markets.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

Stocks of small and medium-sized companies tend to be more vulnerable to adverse developments, may be more volatile, and may be illiquid or restricted as to resale.

The Fund is subject to certain other risks. Please see the current prospectus for more information regarding the risks associated with an investment in the Fund.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management, are based on current market conditions and are subject to change without notice. These opinions may differ from those of other Invesco investment professionals.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Note: Not all products available at all firms. Financial professionals, please contact your home office.

The fund holdings are organized according to the Global Industry Classification Standard, which was developed by and is the exclusive property and service mark of MSCI Inc. and Standard & Poor’s.

* Alpha (cash adjusted) is a measure of performance on a risk-adjusted basis. Beta (cash adjusted) is a measure of relative risk and the slope of regression. Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund’s financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.
